title: Maze Adventures
description: Nine progressively challenging mazes that take students from basic sequencing through loops, conditionals, variables, repeat-until, nested loops, and functions. Guide a robot to the goal by writing programs that grow more elegant at every level.
author: Jason Baddley
objectives: Students will be able to sequence instructions to move a robot through a maze.
Students will be able to use loops to eliminate repeated code and write more elegant solutions.
Students will be able to use conditionals to make decisions based on game state.
Students will be able to store and reuse values with variables.
Students will be able to define and call named procedures to organize reusable code.

standards.description: Model daily processes by creating and following algorithms (sets of step-by-step instructions) to complete tasks.
Create programs that include sequences, events, loops, and conditionals.
Design and iteratively develop programs that combine control structures, including nested loops and compound conditionals.

assessments.question: Why does the ORDER of instructions matter when programming a robot?
options: It doesn't — the computer picks the best order automatically.
Computers follow instructions exactly in order, one at a time.
The robot always goes to the goal, regardless of order.
assessments.rubric: Student demonstrates that computers execute instructions sequentially.

assessments.question: You need to move forward 8 times. Which is the BEST way to write this?
options: Use a 'repeat 8' block with one 'move forward' inside.
Both B and C are equally good.
assessments.rubric: Both B and C demonstrate understanding of loops as a compression of repetition.

assessments.question: What does an 'if' statement do?
options: It repeats a block of code multiple times.
It only runs the code inside it when a condition is true.
It stops the program when something goes wrong.
It makes the robot move in a loop.

assessments.question: Describe in your own words: why would a programmer define a function instead of just copying the same code multiple times?
assessments.rubric: Look for: write once / reuse many times; easier to change; code is shorter and clearer.

levels.name: Robot's first steps
levels.goal: Guide the robot to the star.

prediction.question: If you put just ONE 'move forward' block and press Run, what will happen?
options: The robot zooms all the way to the star
The robot takes one step forward
The robot turns toward the star

hints.1: Look at the wall pattern. What direction does the robot need to go first, and how can you avoid hitting the wall?
hints.2: Try moving east 4 times, then turn right, then south 4 times. Want to use a `repeat 4` block to make it shorter?
hints.3: Drag a `repeat 4` block. Inside it, put one `move forward`. After the repeat, add `turn right`, then another `repeat 4` with a `move forward` inside.

lesson.explanation: Computers follow instructions one at a time, in the exact order you write them — this is called a *sequence*. Change the order, get a different result. It's like a recipe: you crack the egg before you scramble it, never after!
starterQuestions: If I told you to put on your shoes — what would happen if you tried to tie the laces BEFORE putting them on?
Do you think the robot cares about order? What do you think happens if you tell it to turn before it's even moved?
Looking at the maze, what's the very FIRST move the robot should make to get past that wall?
lesson.ahaMoment: Order matters. Computers do exactly what you say, in exactly the order you say it — no more, no less.

levels.name: The long march
levels.goal: Same path as before — but longer. There has to be a faster way…

prediction.question: Without loops, how many 'move forward' blocks would you need to solve this?

hints.1: It's the same shape as last time — just longer. Stacking 12 move blocks works, but is there a tidier way?
hints.2: Try a `repeat 6` with a `move forward` inside. Then turn right. Then another `repeat 6`.
hints.3: Drag a `repeat` block, change the 4 to a 6, drop one `move forward` inside. After it, add `turn right`. Then a second `repeat 6` with a `move forward` inside.

lesson.explanation: When you need to do the same thing many times, a *loop* lets you write it once and run it many times. Instead of dragging 6 move blocks, you drag one `repeat 6` block with a single move inside. Programmers are lazy in a good way — they never write the same thing twice!
starterQuestions: If you had to walk 6 steps, would you rather say 'step, step, step, step, step, step' — or just 'walk 6 steps'?
Count how many times the robot needs to move east on this grid. Does dragging that many blocks sound fun?
What do you think a `repeat 6` block actually does when the program runs — can you picture it?
lesson.ahaMoment: Loops are shortcuts. Find the part that repeats, wrap it in a loop, and your whole program gets shorter and cleaner.

levels.name: Zig-zag
levels.goal: The walls force you to weave. Repeats are your friend.

prediction.question: Count the long straight corridors. How many are there?

hints.1: Trace the path with your finger first. How many segments of 6 moves do you see?
hints.2: East 6, turn right, south 2, turn right, west 6, turn left, south 2, turn left, east 6. Use `repeat 6` for each long run.
hints.3: Three `repeat 6` blocks (each with a `move forward` inside), separated by the right turns. Between them: `turn right` after segment 1, `turn right` then south 2 then `turn right` after segment 2, and `turn left` then south 2 then `turn left` to point back east for segment 3.

lesson.explanation: Every complicated path is actually a pattern in disguise. Once you spot what's repeating, you can describe it with a loop instead of a long list of steps. Programmers call this *abstraction* — seeing the shape of a problem, not just every little detail.
starterQuestions: Before writing a single block, trace the path with your finger. Do you notice any part that looks the same more than once?
How many long corridors does the robot have to run down? Are they all the same length?
If you had to describe this whole maze path in ONE sentence to a friend, what would you say?
lesson.ahaMoment: Complex paths are just simple patterns repeated. Spot the pattern first — then code it.

levels.name: Staircase
levels.goal: Climb the staircase down to the star.

prediction.question: Look at one stair step. How many actions does the robot take to complete it?

hints.1: Each step of the staircase is the same little dance. What two moves repeat?
hints.2: The repeating unit is: turn right, move, turn left, move. Wrap that in a repeat block.
hints.3: Start with one `move forward`. Then a `repeat 5` containing: turn right, move forward, turn left, move forward. Finish with turn right, move forward to land on the star.

lesson.explanation: A loop doesn't have to repeat just one move — it can repeat a whole little *program*. You can put as many blocks as you like inside a loop, and all of them run together, over and over. The stuff inside the loop is called the *loop body*.
starterQuestions: Look at just ONE step of the staircase. What does the robot have to do — how many moves is that?
If the pattern for one stair is four actions, and there are 5 stairs, how many actions is that in total? Would you want to drag them all?
What parts of the solution go INSIDE the loop, and what parts go OUTSIDE of it?
lesson.ahaMoment: A loop body is its own mini-program. Find what repeats as a group, put the whole group inside the loop.

levels.name: Locked door
levels.goal: The door is locked! Grab the key 🔑 first, then open the door ★.

prediction.question: What do you think happens if the robot goes straight to the door without the key?
options: The robot gets stuck — door is locked!

hints.1: The door is locked. What do you need before you can open it — and where is it?
hints.2: Go get the key in the bottom-left corner first. Then head to the door. Use the `if has key` block to wrap the "go to the door" steps.
hints.3: Face south and move down 4 to the key. Then add an `if has key` block. Inside it: turn to face the door (east 2, then north 2) so you only approach the door once you actually hold the key.

lesson.explanation: Sometimes you only want to do something *if* a certain condition is true — that's an `if` statement. It checks something, and only runs the code inside it when the answer is yes. This is how programs make decisions: games check if you won, apps check if you're logged in, and our robot checks whether it's holding the key!
starterQuestions: What do you think happens if the robot goes straight to the door without picking up the key first? Give it a try!
Have you ever seen a locked gate in a video game — one that only opens after you find an item? How did that feel?
The `if has key` block only runs what's inside it when the robot is carrying the key. Can you think of a real-life example of an 'if' rule like that?
lesson.ahaMoment: Conditionals make programs smart. Instead of always doing the same thing, your program can check what's true and decide what to do.

levels.name: Variable Village
levels.goal: Two different distances — can one loop handle both?

prediction.question: The two corridors have different lengths: 6 and 4. How many move blocks WITHOUT loops?

hints.1: The two corridors are different lengths. What if you could store the length in a box, then tell the loop to repeat that many times?
hints.2: Use `set steps to 6`, then `repeat [steps]` to move east. After turning, use `set steps to 4` and `repeat [steps]` again to move south.
hints.3: Drag `set steps to 6`, then a `repeat [steps]` block with one `move forward` inside. Add `turn right`. Then `set steps to 4`, another `repeat [steps]` with one `move forward` inside.

lesson.explanation: A *variable* is like a labelled box that holds a value. You can put a number in it, use it in your program, then change what's in the box and use it again. This means you can write ONE loop that works for ANY distance — just change the variable!
starterQuestions: Imagine you have a box labelled 'steps'. If I write `steps = 6` on a slip of paper and put it in, what does the loop `repeat steps { move }` do?
The two corridors here are different lengths. Would you write two completely different loops — or find a way to reuse one?
What's the difference between writing `repeat 6` and `repeat steps` where `steps = 6`?
lesson.ahaMoment: Variables give names to values. Change the variable, and every place that uses it changes too — write the loop once, use it forever.

levels.name: Fog of War
levels.goal: The corridors are too long to count — find a smarter way to move.

prediction.question: If you use 'repeat 9 {move}' but the corridor is actually 10 cells long, what happens?
options: The robot reaches the end fine
The robot stops one cell too early

hints.1: Counting 9 steps every time is painful. What if there was a block that just kept moving until it couldn't anymore?
hints.2: Use `move forward until wall` for every stretch — including the short south hops. The walls stop the robot at exactly the right spot each time.
hints.3: Five `move until wall` blocks, four turns: move-until-wall, turn right, move-until-wall, turn right, move-until-wall, turn left, move-until-wall, turn left, move-until-wall. The last one lands right on the goal!

lesson.explanation: A *while loop* keeps repeating as long as a condition is true — or until something changes. `Move until wall` is a while loop: it keeps moving forward until it CAN'T anymore. You don't need to know how far — the loop figures it out. This is much more powerful than counting!
starterQuestions: How many steps is each long corridor in this maze? Would you want to type that number manually?
Imagine a robot exploring a building. It has no map — it can't count rooms. How would it know when to stop walking down a corridor?
What's the difference between `repeat 9 { move }` and `move until wall`? Which is safer if the corridor length changes?
lesson.ahaMoment: While loops check a condition instead of counting. They're perfect when you don't know — or don't care — how many times something should repeat.

levels.name: The Snake
levels.goal: Sweep every row — your program must cover the whole grid.

prediction.question: How many times does the 'sweep east, step down, sweep west, step down' pattern repeat?

hints.1: Look at the path row by row. Does the east-then-west pattern repeat? How many times?
hints.2: The outer loop runs 2 times. Each time: move east 4, step south, turn around, move west 4, step south, turn around. Then one final east run to the goal.
hints.3: repeat 2 { repeat 4{move}, turn right, move, turn right, repeat 4{move}, turn left, move, turn left }. Then repeat 4{move} to reach the star.

lesson.explanation: A loop's *body* is just a mini-program — which means it can contain other loops! A *nested loop* is a loop inside a loop: the inner one runs completely for every single step of the outer one. This is how programs draw grids, sweep areas, process tables, and render images.
starterQuestions: Look at the grid row by row — does the robot's movement form a repeating pattern? What's the smallest unit that repeats?
If you had to describe the whole path to a friend in one sentence, what would you say?
Can you put a `repeat 4 { move }` block inside another `repeat` block? What do you think happens?
lesson.ahaMoment: A loop inside a loop: the inner loop completes ALL its rounds for each single step of the outer loop. This is how you describe patterns in two dimensions.

levels.name: Define & Reuse
levels.goal: Create your own named block and reuse it!

prediction.question: If you had to write 'turn right, move, turn left' three times in a row, how many blocks would that be?

hints.1: The robot needs to make the same "step down" move more than once. Could you give that move a name?
hints.2: Define a procedure called "step-down" that contains: turn right, move, turn left. Then call it wherever you need that move.
hints.3: Drag "define my-move". Inside it: turn right, move forward, turn left. Then drag "do my-move" wherever you want to step down.

lesson.explanation: A *function* is a named block of code you define once and use many times. Instead of copying the same moves over and over, you give them a name and 'call' them whenever you need them. In real programming, functions are everywhere — they keep code short and organized.
starterQuestions: If you had to write 'turn right, move, turn left' three times, would you copy it, or would you rather give it a name?
Think about verbs like 'eat breakfast' or 'brush teeth' — those are real-life functions. What moves could you bundle into a function in this maze?
What do you think happens when you 'call' a function — does it happen once or every time you call it?
lesson.ahaMoment: Functions are reusable actions. Name a sequence once, call it everywhere — your code gets shorter and changes in one place apply everywhere.

narrative.title: The Robot's Journey
narrative.synopsis: A robot wakes up in a strange world of mazes. With your help, it learns to move, think, and reason — growing smarter at every challenge until it can navigate any labyrinth on its own.

chapters.chapterSynopsis: The robot opens its eyes for the first time. Every journey begins with a single step — but in what order?
The path ahead is long. Surely there's a smarter way to give all those instructions…
The walls force the robot left and right. Hidden in the chaos is a pattern waiting to be found.
Each stair is the same little dance. Master one step and you've mastered them all.
The goal is in sight — but the door won't budge. The robot must learn to make decisions.
Two paths, two lengths, one loop. The robot discovers the power of named values.
The corridors stretch into darkness — too long to count. The robot learns to trust its senses.
The robot must sweep every row of the grid. A loop inside a loop makes it elegant.
The robot invents its own moves. By naming what it knows, it speaks in a language it created.
